questions.stem: A nurse is providing discharge teaching to a patient newly prescribed levothyroxine (Synthroid) for hypothyroidism. Which instruction is MOST important to include?

options.text: Take the medication with a full meal to improve absorption
Take the medication on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast with water, and avoid taking it with calcium or iron supplements
Double the dose if a dose is missed to maintain thyroid hormone levels
Expect to see symptom improvement within 24-48 hours of starting therapy

questions.rationale: Levothyroxine absorption is significantly reduced by food, calcium, iron supplements, antacids containing calcium/magnesium, and several other medications. The medication should be taken on an empty stomach (30-60 minutes before breakfast) with a full glass of water. Calcium and iron supplements should be taken at least 4 hours apart from levothyroxine. Doubling a missed dose is incorrect and dangerous — skip the missed dose and continue at the regular schedule. Symptom improvement takes weeks to months (TSH normalization is checked at 4-6 weeks after dose adjustment), not 24-48 hours.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "Symptom improvement takes weeks to months (TSH normalization is checked at 4-6 weeks after dose adjustment), not 24-48" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: Because calcium and iron supplements should be taken at least 4 hours apart from levothyroxine suggests medication toxicity, "Double the dose if a dose is missed to maintain thyroid hormone levels" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Expect to see symptom improvement within 24-48 hours of starting therapy" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: Levothyroxine: empty stomach + water + 30-60 min before breakfast. Separate from calcium, iron, antacids by 4h. Never double dose. Onset of therapeutic effect: 4-6 weeks for TSH normalization. Compliance is key — once-daily dosing forever.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration" fits the safer response to it has its own pH and formulation.
distractorRationales.c: "Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration" fits the safer response to the result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" misses the bedside risk because eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation" fits the safer response to a client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: Because the priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse suggests renal injury, "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" would not correct the active problem. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.c: "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" misses the bedside risk because sodium 128 mEq/L points to renal injury. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Teach the client about low-sodium foods" moves care toward teaching or reassurance before renal protection.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Document the event and continue monitoring" misses the bedside risk because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline" fits the safer response to a nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia.
distractorRationales.c: Because chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction suggests circulation or perfusion, "Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Chills, low back pain" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: "Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster" misses the bedside risk because c diff spore precautions infection points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Place the client on droplet precautions only" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.d: Because c diff spore precautions infection suggests infection progression, "Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing" fits the safer response to consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections.
distractorRationales.c: Because dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone suggests infection progression, "Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a client with a chest tube notes continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.text: Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak.
Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes.
Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber.
Strip the tubing toward the drainage system.

questions.rationale: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber suggests an air leak somewhere in the system. The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Prolonged clamping can cause a tension pneumothorax, and stripping the tubing is not recommended because it can increase intrathoracic pressure.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak" fits the safer response to the nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: "Strip the tubing toward the drainage system" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Continuous water-seal bubbling means assess the system for an air leak before doing anything that could trap air in the chest.

questions.stem: A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids. The toes are pale and the client reports numbness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome.
Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose.
Elevate the leg above the level of the heart.
Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Pain out of proportion, pallor, and numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function. Immediate provider notification and urgent evaluation are needed. Comfort-only measures or excessive elevation can worsen ischemia or delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome" fits the safer response to numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "Pain out of proportion, pallor" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome.

questions.stem: A toddler with croup has a barky cough and now has inspiratory stridor at rest. Which finding most concerns the nurse?

options.text: Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing.
Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests.
A barking cough that occurs mainly at night.
Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C).

questions.rationale: Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness alone. The nurse should recognize this as a potential respiratory emergency because increasing work of breathing can quickly progress to fatigue and failure. Mild fever is common and not the most urgent finding here.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: "A barking cough that occurs mainly at night" misses the bedside risk because stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: Because stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C)" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: In croup, stridor at rest is the red-flag sign of worsening airway obstruction.
